figures.caption: Figure 4 TRIBE v2 recovers, in-silico, a variety of findings in visual neuroscience. TRIBE v2 is tested on various visual functional localizers, each extracted from the Individual Brain Charting (IBC) dataset (Pinho et al., 2018). Experimental images are flashed for 1 second, with an interval of 8 seconds, and a general linear model (GLM) is fit on the predicted time-series to obtain z-scored contrast maps (see section 5.9 for details). A. Mean evoked response to the images of the FaceBody task. B. Examples of images from each category. The images are taken from the FaceBody and Visu tasks from the IBC dataset. C. Contrast maps obtained from TRIBE’s predictions (see section 5.9). D. Contrast maps computed on the IBC dataset, averaged across participants and trials. E. Numerical agreement between ground truth and predicted z-scores. Each dot represents one of the 360 parcels from the HCP parcellation (Glasser et al., 2016), and z-scores are averaged for all cortical vertices falling within the parcel. Functionally relevant parcels are indicated in colors, with the marker disambiguating the hemisphere. Higher correlations between true and predicted brain activity patterns indicate better zero-shot in-silico experiments.
Figure 8 Performance of TRIBE across cortical regions.
Figure 9 Examples of decoded BOLD timeseries for various areas of the brain.

Figure 1 Approach. A. The brain activity of healthy volunteers was recorded with functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI), while they were presented to naturalistic (movies, podcasts) and experimental conditions (e.g. flashed objects, isolated words). B. TRIBE v2 is trained to predict high-resolution fMRI from any audio, video, and/or text stimuli, using embeddings from pretrained AI models Grattafiori et al. (2024); Chung et al. (2021); Assran et al. (2025). C. Example of the average brain response (mean across 176 subjects) to a movie clip (Van Essen et al., 2013), and the corresponding zero-shot prediction from TRIBE v2.

Figure 5 TRIBE v2 recovers, in-silico, a variety of classic findings in neurolinguistics. TRIBE v2 is tested with various language functional localizer tasks, extracted from the Individual Brain Charting (IBC) dataset (Pinho et al., 2018). A. Mean evoked response prediction to ten-word sentences in the "simple" condition of the RSVP task. B. In-silico experiment categories. These conditions are extracted from the Bang, ArchiSocial, EmotionalPain and RSVP tasks from IBC. C. Contrast maps obtained from TRIBE’s predictions (see section 5.9). D. Contrast maps computed on the IBC dataset, averaged across participants and trials. E. Numerical agreement between ground truth and predicted z-scores. Each dot represents one of the 360 parcels from the HCP parcellation (Glasser et al., 2016), and z-scores are averaged for all cortical vertices falling within the parcel. Functionally relevant parcels are indicated in colors, with the marker disambiguating the hemisphere.
Table 1 Characteristics of the datasets. Wide datasets prioritize population-level scale and generalizability, whereas deep datasets prioritize individual-level granularity and precision.

Figure 6 Independent component analysis (ICA) shows that TRIBE v2 learns neuroscientifically relevant patterns. A. Top. TRIBE v2’s ICA. Bottom semantic maps for five neuroscientific keywords generated by Neurosynth (Kent et al., 2026). In both cases, the 10% vertices with the highest values are shown. B. Spatial correlation (R) between each ICA component and the functional networks to well-known functional networks.

Figure 2 TRIBE v2 predicts fMRI responses accurately across the whole brain. A. Encoding scores in various important regions of the cortex as defined by the HCP parcellation (see App. figure 8 for more details), color-coded in the cortical surface on the left. Error bars denote Standard Error of the Mean (SEM) across the participants of all studies. B. Encoding scores in the 8 subcortical regions considered, color-coded in the figure on the left. Error bars denote SEM across the participants of all studies. C. Subject-averaged encoding scores across cortical and subcortical regions for the four conditions in the training dataset. D. Average encoding scores of individual subject (dots) for TRIBE v2 and a classic linear baseline. E. Scaling laws of brain encoding. Average encoding score of TRIBE v2 on the Algonauts dataset, as we increase the number of hours of training data used per participant. Error bar indicates the SEM across subjects.

Figure 10 Examples of video segments and the corresponding predicted and true brain responses. In all cases, the brain activity is normalized to the 99% percentile across the segment. See more examples in our interactive demo https://aidemos.atmeta.com/tribev2/.

Figure 7 TRIBE v2 reveals insights into themultimodality of the human brain. A. Average cortical encoding scores of TRIBE v2 when trained on subsets of the three modalities for the Algonauts dataset. Error bars denote SEM across the four participants. B. Highest scoring modality across the whole cortex, with red, green and blue depicting text, audio and video respectively. C. Difference between the encoding score of TRIBE v2 and the best of its unimodal counterparts. D. Each vertex of the cortical and subcortical sufaces are color-coded using an RGB mapping where red, green and blue intensities are determined by the subject-averaged encoding score of the text, audio and video unimodal versions of TRIBE v2 on the Algonauts dataset. Red, blue and green areas correspond to unimodal areas well encoded by text, audio and video respectively, while magenta, yellow and cyan correspond to bimodal areas well encoded by text+video, text+audio and video+audio respectively. Saturation is normalized to make colors more readable. E. Same as D for each of the four subjects in the Algonauts dataset.

Figure 3 TRIBE v2 generalizes zero-shot to new tasks and subjects, and can be finetuned on a small amount of data to improve individualized predictions. A. Left: Pearson correlation between (i) the brain response to speech of an individual subject k and (ii) the average brain response of the rest of the cohort. Each dot represents a subject. The bars represent the correlation between the average cohort response and the zero-shot predictions from TRIBE v2 (green) and its linear counterpart (red). Right: cortical map of the scores achieved, averaged across datasets. B. Same as A but for videos instead of speech. C. Left: encoding scores of TRIBE v2 for all subjects of the unseen speech datasets (each dot represents a subject and the horizontal lines represent the mean). We compare the results obtained with a linear model (either zero-shot or trained from scratch) to those obtained with TRIBE v2 (either zero-shot or finetuned on half of the unseen subjects’ data). Right: cortical map of the scores achieved, averaged across datasets. D. Same as C but for videos instead of speech.
